reasoning: The SNDR savings rate is currently flat at 0.41% as the Fed maintains its policy rate. In the short term (April 2025), a rate cut is not anticipated, so the rate will likely remain anchored at 0.41%. There is a small historical probability (around 15%) of a 1 basis point random statistical bounce to 0.42% during a hold. Further out (June and September), the CME FedWatch tool and FOMC projections expect up to two rate cuts by the end of 2025. This would typically push the savings rate down to the 0.38-0.40% range, significantly reducing the median expectation. However, as the forecast horizon expands out to late 2025 and March 2026, macroeconomic uncertainty (e.g. potential inflation surprises from new policies) broadens the distribution of outcomes. The increasing variance leads to a fatter right tail where the Fed might have to reverse course and hike rates again. Therefore, while the median expectation is down, the probability of exceeding the exact 0.41% threshold naturally increases toward 0.5 as the time horizon extends, perfectly in line with both structural uncertainty and the given forecasting instructions.
